title: What the Buddha Taught
subtitle: Revised and Expanded Edition
author: Walpola Rahula
themes: The Empirical Nature of Buddhist Teaching
Compassion as Natural Expression of Wisdom
excerpt: The finest single-volume introduction to the Buddha's original teaching as preserved in the Pali Canon. Walpola Rahula, a Buddhist monk and scholar, presents the Four Noble Truths, the Noble Eightfold Path, anatta (non-self), karma, rebirth, and nirvana with exceptional clarity, authority, and fidelity to the earliest sources.
keyIdeas: Buddhism is not a speculative philosophy but a practical path to liberation based on the empirical analysis of human suffering and its cessation
The Four Noble Truths form the entire doctrine: suffering exists, it has a cause, it can be ended, and there is a path to end it — the Noble Eightfold Path
The Buddha rejected both eternalism (an unchanging soul) and nihilism (annihilation after death), teaching instead the middle way of dependent origination
Anatta (non-self) is the most distinctive and challenging Buddhist concept: there is no permanent, unchanging self or soul, only a constantly changing stream of psycho-physical processes
Karma in Buddhism is not fate or destiny but volitional action driven by intention, and its results can be modified through mindfulness and ethical living
Nirvana is not a place or a state of nothingness but the unconditioned reality that can be realised here and now through the extinction of greed, hatred, and delusion
keyTakeaways: The Buddha taught a middle way between indulgence and asceticism, accessible to all people regardless of background
The Four Noble Truths are not beliefs to be accepted on faith but a diagnostic framework to be investigated and verified through direct experience
Meditation is not optional in Buddhism — it is the primary tool for developing the insight that leads to liberation
The Buddha's teaching is emphatically empirical: 'Come and see' (ehipassiko), not 'believe because I say so'
Buddhist ethics are rooted in compassion and non-harm, not obedience to divine commands
The distinction between Buddhism as religion and Buddhism as philosophy is a Western imposition that the tradition itself does not recognise
whoShouldRead: Anyone seeking a reliable, non-sectarian introduction to the Buddha's original teachings
Meditation practitioners who want to understand the doctrinal foundation of their practice
Students of comparative religion and philosophy
Western Buddhists who want to go beyond popular mindfulness to the core of the tradition
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a guide to meditation practice — the book explains theory, not technique
Those interested primarily in Zen, Tibetan, or other Mahayana traditions — this is a Theravada text
People who want a critical or secular reinterpretation of Buddhism — Rahula presents traditional orthodoxy
